text: Exercise-based injury prevention programs do not significantly reduce the overall risk or rate of running-related injuries in endurance runners based on pooled meta-analytic results.
Supervised exercise-based intervention programs significantly reduce the risk of running-related injuries compared to unsupervised or running-only controls.
Supervision may be essential for exercise-based injury prevention programs to be effective in runners, likely because it increases compliance with the prescribed exercises.
Running-related injuries are a leading cause of dropout from the sport and represent a substantial financial burden to runners and healthcare services.
The majority of studies investigating exercise-based injury prevention in runners are of low methodological quality, limiting the certainty of current evidence.
